coredata.dc:description: A demand exists for the development of miniaturized biosensors with high sensitivity and a wide dynamic range for rapid detecting dopamine (DA) levels. In this work, an electrochemical sensor is created by decorating closely packed cobalt oxide (Co3O4) nanograins on nitrogen-doped electrospun carbon nanofibers (NECNFs) via a facile electrodeposition method. The carbon nanofiber matrix provides both a scaffold with ultrahigh surface area for homogeneous dispersion of cobalt oxide nanostructures and facilitates the electrochemical conductivity for the hybrid electrode. The sensing principle is based on the redox reaction between DA and DA quinone that generates electrical signal, which is detectable via differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and chronoamperometry (CA). Owing to a high electroactive surface area and facilitating electron transfer between electrode surface and the target molecule, the electrical sensing platform exhibits superior sensitivity and selectivity toward DA, with an excellent limit of detection (9 nM) over a wide range of concentrations (0.01 to 100 µM). Moreover, based on its excellent sensing properties and biocompatibility, the sensor has been used for the real-time monitoring exogeneous DA released from pheochromocytoma (PC12) cells. This work demonstrates promise of a novel sensor technology that may render rapid point-of-care testing of DA towards early clinical diagnosis.

XPS survey spectra (a) Co2p spectra (b), O1s spectra (c), and Raman spectra (d) of NECNFs (black), 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (blue), and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(b) Nyquist plots of GCE (black) NENCFs (green), 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (blue), and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs (red) electrodes. (b) DPV measurements of the 4h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode with a varied DA concentration (0.05 to 100 µM). (c) Calibration curve of the peak current of the 4h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode with corresponding DA concentration. (d) Differential pulse voltammograms of the 4h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode for detection of 0.01 mM DA (red, line), 0.04 mM UA (blue, line), and 0.04 mM AA (orange, line) separately and together (pink dotted line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration of electrocatalytic reactions of DA and the selectivity on the nanohybrid electrode.

(b) Fluorescence images of live (green) and dead (red) stained PC12 cells cultured on the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs for 24, 48, and 72 h. (c) Amperometric responses of the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode with (red) and without (blue) PC12 cells in 0.1 M PBS upon addition of 120 mM KCl, as wells as with PC12 cells in the absence of K+ (black). head.ce:title: Manipulating cobalt oxide on N-doped aligned electrospun carbon nanofibers towards instant electrochemical detection of dopamine secreted by living cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A demand exists for the development of miniaturized biosensors with high sensitivity and a wide dynamic range for rapid detecting dopamine (DA) levels. In this work, an electrochemical sensor is created by decorating closely packed cobalt oxide (Co3O4) nanograins on nitrogen-doped electrospun carbon nanofibers (NECNFs) via a facile electrodeposition method. The carbon nanofiber matrix provides both a scaffold with ultrahigh surface area for homogeneous dispersion of cobalt oxide nanostructures and facilitates the electrochemical conductivity for the hybrid electrode. The sensing principle is based on the redox reaction between DA and DA quinone that generates electrical signal, which is detectable via differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and chronoamperometry (CA). Owing to a high electroactive surface area and facilitating electron transfer between electrode surface and the target molecule, the electrical sensing platform exhibits superior sensitivity and selectivity toward DA, with an excellent limit of detection (9 nM) over a wide range of concentrations (0.01 to 100 µM). Moreover, based on its excellent sensing properties and biocompatibility, the sensor has been used for the real-time monitoring exogeneous DA released from pheochromocytoma (PC12) cells. This work demonstrates promise of a novel sensor technology that may render rapid point-of-care testing of DA towards early clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite significant advances in the electrochemical detection of small molecular metabolites, a growing need exists for the development of portable point-of-care medical products with miniaturized portable electrochemical sensing systems [1]. A majority of electrical biosensors, whose electrode surface materials with high electrical conductivity, electron mobility, and electrocatalytic properties, holds considerable promise for clinical diagnosis and continuous monitoring of complex human health conditions; such sensor systems are currently implemented for recording glucose levels [2,3], monitoring serotonin levels [4,5], and determining hydrogen peroxide content [6,7], amongst others. As such, the development of new sensors and the miniaturization of existing sensors, or the amount of a sample required to yield diagnostic information, is of great interest to the field of electrical bioelectronics.

Cobalt oxide is attractive as a sensor material because of its good chemical durability, promising surface structure for facilitating catalytic activity, and its ability to perform fast reversible redox reactions [8,9]. In addition, cobalt oxides are classified as a low toxic chemical substance with relatively good biocompatibility [10]. The utility of cobalt oxide for electrochemical sensing is often limited because of relatively poor capacitance and slow electron transfer kinetics [11], however, previous studies have shown that the formation of hybridized cobalt oxide-based nanomaterials with doping or composites with a heterointerface on conductive materials, such as carbon nanotubes [12], carbon nanofibers [13] and metal nanosheets [14], can alleviate these issues. In this work, we coupled cobalt oxide with carbon fiber electrodes.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have their unique chemical structure, where the sp2 hybridization combined carbon atoms generate delocalization of electrons [15]. Owing to these features, they exhibit excellent electrical properties including electron conductivity and mobility to accelerate the redox reaction in electrochemical sensing. Moreover, one-dimensional (1D) carbon nanomaterials, including fibers, tubes, wires, and rods, exhibit extraordinary properties for sensing; namely efficient electron transport, high electrical conductivities, and large surface-to-volume ratios [16]. Carbon-fiber based microelectrodes are particularly advantageous for bioelectronic sensors because of their light weight, high tensile strength, flexibility, and affordability [17,18]. A conventional strategy for fabricating 1D nanomaterials is electrospinning which can be used to produce large scale materials with a defined surface area and morphology [19–22]. Introduction of other components into or on the surface of the carbon-fibers can be used to further control their electrochemical characteristics and hence performance [13,23,24]. For instance, charge exchange between heteroatom dopants and the carbon framework has been shown to modulate electronic structure characteristics and affect catalytic performance [25]. Incorporation of nitrogen-containing functional groups, such as pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen, into the carbon matrix allows for the adsorption and immobilization of metal atoms or nanoparticles and can be used to further tailor the material’s characteristics [26]. For example, previous experiments have shown that the covalent attachment of metal oxide nanoparticles onto a carbon matrix increases the conductivity and electrochemical activity of the composite system [27]. Co-doping the material with other different heteroatoms, including N/B, N/P, N/S, N/F and N/O, has also been used to improve the reaction kinetics and catalytic activity [28].

The sensor platform reported here is designed for the detection of dopamine (DA). DA is an essential neurotransmitter of the catecholamine family and plays a paramount role in regulating various physiological activities in the central nervous, renal, cardiovascular, and endocrine systems [29,30]. Changes in DA concentration and its spatial and temporal kinetics can indicate neurodegenerative disorders, such as Parkinson’s disease and schizophrenia [31,32]. Moreover, abnormal levels of DA, in either urine or blood, can indicate the presence of pheochromocytomas and paragangliomas, which are rare and imperceptible tumors arising in neural crest tissue [33]. Currently, there is not an effective serological test for Parkinson’s diseases or schizophrenia. Therefore, monitoring DA levels in the serum or bodily fluids offers insight for doctors to determine if a patient either has a neurotransmitter expressing tumor and / or the efficacy of a neurotransmitter drug. Traditional methods for the detection of DA include, fluorescence spectroscopy [34,35], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [36], and high-performance liquid chromatography [37]; however, these approaches require sophisticated instruments, time-consuming analysis, appropriately trained technicians, and complex preparation procedures [38,39], which can hinder wide-scale applicability. By contrast, electrochemical sensing of DA offers the potential for real-time and in situ detection [40–42]. However, the coexistence of other reactive species, such as AA (ascorbic acid) and UA (uric acid), which have similar redox properties to DA, leading to a nested signal in DA analysis, can degrade sensor selectivity. To account for interferents, these studies engineered a nanohybrid sensor with nitrogen dopants which introduces excess negative charge on the surface-active sites and inhibits detection of the negatively charged AA and UA [30,43].

In this study, electrospun carbon nanofibers (ECNFs) with uniformly distributed nitrogen-doped functional groups are used to guide a low current electrodeposition of cobalt oxide and form the Co3O4@NECNF composites. The studies presented herein demonstrate the rational design of a novel electrochemical sensor by integrating Co3O4 nanograins on nitrogen doped aligned ECNFs. The sensor exhibits superb catalytic performance towards the oxidation of DA, showing sensitive discrimination of DA from other coexisting molecules, and provides the capability of in-situ real-time detection of DA from the living dopaminergic cells, pheochromocytoma (PC12). These findings illustrate the potential applicability of electrochemical sensors as an implantable, portable, miniaturized, and point-of-care clinical diagnostic device in living tissues.

A Carl Zeiss Auriga-BU FIB field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) was used to examine the topographies of the samples. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by an Agilent Technologies Oxford Gemini X-Ray Diffractometer equipped with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm). The Raman spectra were collected using a Horiba XploRA One Raman Confocal Microscope System with a 785 nm laser as the excitation source. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on an XPS-ESCALAB Xi + Thermo Scientific instrument using an Al K-α radiation source. The binding energies of spectra were calibrated to a C1s peak at 284.8 eV. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) measurements were performed using an ESCALAB 250XI XPS instrument with a He(I) discharge lamp. A −5.0 V bias was applied to the stage and a pass energy of 3.5 eV was used during collection. All electrochemical experiments were carried out using a Bio-Logic VMP3 electrochemical workstation, with a conventional three-electrode system. Electrodeposition, cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) measurements were performed using the electrochemical workstation. A 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (Invitrogen) at pH 7.4 was used as the supporting electrolyte, unless otherwise specified. All solutions were prepared using deionized (DI) water and deoxygenated by bubbling with nitrogen for 20 min before performing DA electrocatalytic measurements.

An electrospinning method was used to fabricate the pristine well-aligned ECNFs using a polymer solution of 10 wt% polyacrylonitrile (PAN, MW = 150,000, ACROS Organics) in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, Sigma Aldrich). A self-designed sample collector, with four steel poles welded on a plate, was employed to collect the ECNFs without any substrates, according to our previously reported method and process [44–46]. A high voltage of 18 kV was applied between the needle and the collector, which were located 15 cm apart from each other. During the electrospinning, the collector was maintained at a rate of 2000 revolutions per minute, while the ECNFs precursor was fed at a rate of 1 mL h−1 for five hours using a syringe pump. The PAN sheets were then put into a furnace (Oxidation and Annealing Furnace) for the formation of the graphitic carbon structure. The heating rate was 1 °C min−1 from room temperature to 280 °C and then kept for 6 h in air. Next, the stabilized nanofibers were carbonized at a ramping rate of 5 °C min−1 up to 1200 °C for 1 h under a nitrogen atmosphere to yield high mechanical strength well-aligned ECNFs.

Following fabrication of the pristine ECNFs, the material was activated by immersion in 8 M nitric acid solution (HNO3, J.T. Baker) for 2 h to promote the nitrogen-doping. The activated ECNFs (AECNFs) were placed in a Teflon autoclave reactor (50 mL volume, PPL-lined vessel chamber kettle, 250 °C), filled with 25 mL of 1 M ammonia solution (Sigma Aldrich), and heated at 180 °C for 5 h to dope the ECNFs with nitrogen (NECNF). The NECNFs were then rinsed with DI water and dried using nitrogen gas. The nitrogen doping was confirmed using XPS; see Fig. S1 for survey spectra upon doping. Cobalt oxide was then electrodeposited onto a 1 cm2 NECNF substrate using a three-electrode configuration at a constant current of 55 μA for either 2 h or 4 h. To apply the potential to the NECNFs, a gold electrode was used as a base and the NECNFs were taped on top using conductive carbon glue (TED PELLA, INC). An aqueous solution containing 40 mM CoSO4 (ACROS Organics) and 100 mM NaSO4 (ACROS Organics) under a nitrogen atmosphere was used as the electrodeposition solution. The obtained nanohybrids, Co3O4@NECNFs, were then rinsed with DI water and dried at 80 °C for 3 h.

A Bio-Logic VMP3 electrochemical workstation was used to carry out the electrochemical characterization and measurement in a three-electrode configuration [3]. The above prepared Co3O4@NECNFs electrode was cut into an appropriate size. The working electrode was a 5 mm × 5 mm Co3O4@NECNFs flake and it was placed onto a glassy carbon electrode using conducting carbon glue, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, and a platinum wire was used as the counter electrode. Cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy measurements were carried out to record the electrochemical performance of the nanohybrid electrode in 5 mM [Fe(CN6)]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl solution. Differential pulse voltammetry and chronoamperometry methods were performed to detect the analyte DA. Electrochemical monitoring of DA was conducted in the three-electrode system containing 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) following high purity nitrogen bubbling for 20 min.

The PC12 cell line was selected to investigate regulated neurotransmitter secretion from neurons and was obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC®CRL-1721TM). We chose the suspension PC12 cells, a type of catecholamine cells, which synthesize, store and release DA. And it is recommended and extensively characterized in neurosecretion.[47] The cells were cultured in optimized Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, ATCC®30-2002TM) supplemented with 10% horse serum (Sigma Aldrich), 5 % fetal bovine serum (Sigma Aldrich), and 1% streptomycin-penicillin (Fisher Scientific), respectively. Cells were grown in a humidified incubator with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C and passaged the cells twice a week. All experiments were performed on cells between passage 15 and 25.

The cytotoxicity of the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrid electrodes was investigated by a standard Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. The cells (2 × 105 cell/mL) were seeded onto a 96-well plate with a total volume of 100 μL/well and maintained under a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The modified NECNF nanohybrids were sterilized in 70% ethanol in petri dishes for 10 min, thereafter, most of the ethanol was removed by evaporating for 20 min. Then, the samples were cut into an appropriate size and pre-wetted with cell culture medium for approximately 10 min prior to transfer to the middle of the seeding well plate for several hours. The 10 µL CCK-8 solution (Sigma Aldrich) was added into each well and incubated for 2 h in the CO2 incubator. The viability measurements of the cells were carried out by measuring the absorbance at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Bio Tek Microplate reader).

To further evaluate the biocompatibility of the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrids, the PC12 cells, at a density of 1.7 × 106 cells/mL, were seeded onto the nanohybrid and placed in the middle of a confocal dish where the surface of the sample was coated with 0.1 mg/ml poly D-lysine solution (Sigma Aldrich). The cells were then co-inoculated with the presence of the nanohybrid composite which was placed on a confocal dish containing a cell culture medium volume of 300 µL. Next, the confocal dish was placed into an incubator for 24 h and the growth medium was replaced with live/dead cell dye solution, composed of calcein AM (1 µL) and ethidium homodimer-1 (4 µL) dissolved in 2 mL sterilized phosphate buffer saline. Following 20 min of incubation the stained cells were then imaged under a confocal microscope (Zeiss Z1 Spinning Disk Confocal Microscope) at 20 × magnification. The live cells uptake calcein AM and were stained in green, which was observed with the FITC channel, whereas dead cells uptake ethidium homodimer-1 and were stained in red, observed by the RHOD channel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time in situ electrochemical detection of DA secreted by living cells
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have shown that a high concentration of potassium stimulates membrane depolarization of the PC12 cells and causes the secretion of DA by exocytosis.[48,49] The spikes of DA oxidation occur with a latency less than 1 s after membrane depolarization from the high extracellular potassium concentration and the peak current decreased upon successive stimulation due to the depletion of cellular DA stores [42]. It is assumed that the in situ detected peak current, originating from released DA, depends critically on the number of cells in the system and the diffusion distance between the cells and the electrode [42]. To account for diffusion, the Co3O4@NECNFs electrode was placed near living cells and chronoamperometric methods were carried out under 0.3 V to record the current response of DA secreted by PC cells where potassium chloride (120 mM) was injected into 7 mL 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 8 × 106 cells, determined by the cell counter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and characterization of the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrid electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: Three-step synthesis process for cobalt oxide nanograins decorated onto nitrogen-doped ECNFs is shown in Scheme 1 . The pristine ECNFs was pretreated with nitric acid to enrich epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl functional surface groups with good hydrophilicity. Specifically, nitrogen dopants were introduced into the carbon framework by hydrothermal treatment under ammonia solution. In the NECNFs template, the cobalt-salt precursor was seeded onto it via facile electrodeposition (see details in experimental section). In the end, Co3O4 nuclei were homogenously anchored to the surface of NECNFs via attractive electrostatic forces and grew under the constant current [44].

Theoretical calculations on nanocarbon materials with dopants indicate that synergistic effects between the dopant and scaffold can lead to stronger binding strength of metal ions for nucleation and growth [50]. The nucleation of cobalt oxide on the NECNFs has been shown previously to occur through the formation of C-O-metal and C-N-metal bonds between the NENCFs and cobalt atoms [27]. Successful doping of nitrogen into the ECNFs carbon framework was confirmed by XPS; see the survey spectra shown in Fig. S1. The surface chemical oxidation of the pristine ECNFs using nitric acid activates the ECNFs (AECNFs) with increased defects from O dopants. Fig. S2 shows C 1s and O 1s XPS spectra of the AECNFs and indicates that the surface comprises carboxyl, ether, and hydroxyl groups. The oxygen-containing functional groups endow the carbon framework with more hydrophilicity and provide chemical functionalities that bind other catalysts, such as the subsequent heteroatom dopants and metal ions [28]. Upon treatment of the AECNFs with ammonia (see methods for more details), the nitrogen adheres to the defect sites and the AECNFs become N-doped (NECNF). Fig. 1 (a) shows high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra of the NECFNs. The spectra were fit to a sum of three characteristic peaks and are associated with graphitic nitrogen (gN, blue), pyrrolic nitrogen (rN, green), and pyridinic nitrogen (pN, orange); see SI for details on the peak fitting and stoichiometry in Table S1. Additionally, C 1s and O 1s spectra in Fig. S3 indicate that the surface functional groups found in the AECNFs, (carboxyls, ethers, and hydroxyls) persist upon N-doping.

The role of N and O doping in the nucleation and subsequent growth of cobalt oxide during electrodeposition is suspected to occur in an analogous manner to other works [26,51–53]. The extra electron density, arising from the rN, gN, and the lone pair on pN, act as electron donors to the ECNF π-system and as Lewis base sites which couple strongly to the acidic cobalt ions in solution [25,26,50]. Moreover, the oxygen containing surface functional groups can act as binding sites which further facilitate cobalt electrodeposition and growth. The degree of surface modification for the different treatments was determined using Raman spectroscopy. Fig. 1(b) shows Raman spectra for the ECNFs (black), AECNFs (red), and NECNFs (blue) samples. Two characteristic peaks are observed at ∼1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1 and are assigned to the D-band and G-band, respectively. Previous reports have attributed the G-band to in-plane carbon–carbon stretching modes consistent with a graphitic structure and the D-band to defects and disorder in the carbon nanofiber [51]. Therefore, the intensity ratio of the two bands (ID/IG) indicates a relative amount of defects for the material, which was analyzed by fitting the Raman spectra with a Lorentzian line-shape function. Intensity ratios of 1.02, 1.05, and 1.06 were found for the ECNFs, AECNFs, and NECNFs respectively, suggesting that successive treatments cause an increase in defect sites. These findings are further corroborated by XRD studies, where the peak position at 2θ = 26.5, corresponds to the (002) reflection of graphitic-type lattice of different modification of ECNFs. Herein, much broader peak reflections in both AECNFs and NECNFs stem from the variation of dislocation density and correlations between dislocations [54], as seen in Fig. S4. The emergence of more defects sites aids in the nucleation and subsequent growth of cobalt oxide and correlates with an enhancement of the electrochemical performance, vide infra.

Because doping and functionalization of the carbon framework affect the surface electron distribution, and subsequently the band gap, surface affinity, and catalytic properties [55], an appropriate characterization of the electronic structure was necessary. Work function measurements, corresponding to the energy required to remove an electron from the Fermi level to vacuum, were employed using ultraviolet photoemission spectroscopy (UPS) and are shown in Fig. S5. The work function of the ENCFs, AECNFs, and NECNFs were found to be 4.6, 4.5, and 3.8 eV, respectively. The stark difference in work function upon N-doping indicates that a change in the charge density and electronic structure occurs and most likely arises from dipolar contributions and electron donation to the graphitic lattice [56]. The decrease in work function is expected to play a role in facilitating electron transfer for both nucleation of the cobalt oxide and in electrocatalytic applications.

The surface morphology of the carbon nanofiber nanohybrids was characterized by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). Fig. 2 (a) and (d) show the smooth surface structure with a well-aligned orientation for the NECNFs. Electrodeposition of cobalt oxide was then performed at a fixed current density of 55 µA cm−2 yielding the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrid materials. Following 2 h of electrodeposition, nucleation and the uniform growth of spherical cobalt oxide nanograins appears on the surface with an average nanograin size of 162 ± 25 nm; see Fig. 2(b) & (e) and Fig. S6. Upon further electrodeposition time (4 h), the nanograins grow in size to 350 ± 75 nm (Fig. 2(c) & (f) and Fig. S6). Introduction of heteroatoms with nitrogen dopants and oxygen-containing functional groups in the carbon skeleton play key roles in the formation of the three-dimensional nanostructures. Control experiments for the electrodeposition of cobalt oxide on AECNFs are shown in Fig. S7 and they display more monolithic features, owing to the lack of nitrogen sites for the nucleation and growth of nanograins. The fractures of Co3O4 were observed for the 4-hour electrodeposition which may be due to the N-dopant active sites for longer time Co3O4 growth from the nanograins in the seed-mediated island which could be described with Frank–van der Merwe mode in the electrolyte solution [57], agreeing with a previous report on fracture formation during electrodeposition of metal hydroxide or oxide [58]. The fractures can largely increase the surface area for catalyzing DA and facilitate access of the DA to active sites and the charge transfer between the solution and the electrode [59].

Fig. 3 (a) shows XPS survey spectra of the NECNFs before (black) and after 2 h (blue) and 4 h (red) of cobalt oxide electrodeposition. Signals from the N1s photoelectrons are attenuated in the two samples following electrodeposition presumably because the cobalt oxide tends to grow at these sites. Fig. 3(b) shows the Co 2p core-level XPS spectra for the two different electrodeposition times. Both spectra display two main peaks at 780.8 eV and 795.5 eV, corresponding to the Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 doublet and a satellite structure consistent with the formation of Co3O4 [60]. Fig. 3(c) shows O1s XPS spectra for 2-hours (2 h) Co3O4@NECNFs (blue), and 4-hour (4 h) Co3O4@NECNFs (red). For the NECNFs two main peaks are observed (Fig. S3(b)): Ox at 531.5 eV which corresponds to surface oxygen vacancies and/or oxygen-containing groups [61] and Oy at 533.2 eV associated with chemically or physically absorbed water molecules [62]. Upon electrodeposition, a new peak emerges at 529.6 eV, labeled Oz, and arises from a metal–oxygen bond [14].

Raman spectroscopy was performed to further characterize the microstructure of the cobalt oxide nanoparticles on the surface of the NECNFs; Fig. 3d shows the emergence of four Raman bands on 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (blue) and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs (red) compared to the bare NECNFs (Fig. 1b). The peaks at 460, 508, 604, and 663 cm−1 are consistent with the reported values of the Eg, F2 2g, F3 2g, and A1g modes of crystalline Co3O4 [63–66]. The D-band and G-band peaks of the NECNFs are concomitant with these Raman bands from crystalline Co3O4, indicating a successful combination of the carbon matrix with the metal oxide nanoparticles. Fig. S8 shows XRD patterns of the two carbon nanofiber hybrids with different electrodeposition time. Both samples agree with a standard XRD pattern (JCPDS no. 43–1003), accompanied by some broadened characteristic peaks and a strengthened (111) peak [67]. The diffraction peaks of Co3O4 were not obvious most likely due to the inhomogeneity of small size particles embedded on carbon nanofibers [68].

The implantation of nitrogen atoms and nitrogen containing functional groups offers abundant binding sites and strong coupling to anchor cobalt ions and is favorable for the in situ nucleation and growth of cobalt oxide nanomaterials [11]. The nitrogen-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the ECNFs trap electrons around the defect sites; and they can promote the reactions [53,64]. The positively charged cobalt ions then absorb onto the surface of the ECNFs, where the absorbed substances act as preferential nucleation sites for in situ growth of larger crystals. The three-dimensional cobalt oxide nanostructures display high surface area, which is expected to enhance the number of exposed active sites, provide good electrolyte contact, and facilitate ion/charge transport and diffusion for electrochemical sensing [69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrid electrode materials
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) shows cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements of glassy carbon (GCE, black), NENCFs (green), 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (blue), and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs (red) using a standard redox couple, ferri-ferrocyanides, to check key parameters affecting the charge transfer along with the designated electrode surfaces, including charge transport, electrocatalytic activity and surface chemistry. Two pairs of redox peaks were clearly observed, where one pair of them at lower potential belongs to the ferri/ferrocyanide redox probe; and the other one at higher potential is the redox reaction of the cobalt species. An increase in both faradaic and capacitive current was observed for NECNF substrates upon electrodeposition of cobalt oxide, with the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs exhibiting the highest peak current, indicating a higher electrochemical active surface area. In addition to the change in peak current, a significant decrease in the anodic and cathodic peak separation, ∼150 mV, is observed for samples with electrodeposited cobalt oxide and is concomitant with an increase in the electron transfer rate. Thus, results show that 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs exhibit an efficient electron transfer activity by contrast to that of bare GCE. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to further explore the electrochemical kinetics occurring at the electrode surface / solution interface. Fig. 4(b) shows Nyquist plots for bare GCE (black), NECNFs (green), 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (blue), and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs (red) electrodes. For bare GCE and NECNFs electrodes only one semicircle at high frequency is observed and corresponds to a charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of 332 Ω and 139 Ω, respectively; and the equivalent circuit model for the Nyquist plot is shown in Fig. S9. For the Co3O4@NECNFs, two semicircles are observed; corresponding to a Randles circuit model which is shown in Fig. S10. In this model, the high-frequency semicircle is attributed to electrolyte resistance (R∞) from an interfacial layer between the electrolyte and electrode [70]; while the low-frequency semicircle is associated with the sum of charge transfer resistance and/or mass transfer resistance (Rmt) [71]. Here we focused on the second semicircle for examining the charge transfer properties. The decrease in resistance for the Rct/Rmt for 2 h Co3O4@NECNFs (89 Ω) and 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs Rct/Rmt (35 Ω) compared to NECNFs indicates that cobalt oxide acts to facilitate charge transport and separation, in agreement with previous reports [12]. Herein, the presence of a second semicircle is primarily owing to decoration of cobalt oxide on the surface of NECNFs, implying additional interface layer for redox species passing through. The excellent electrochemical performance is attributed to the carbon layer between the cobalt oxide nanostructures, which acts as a conductive support to reduce the internal resistance and enhance electrical conductivity [72]. Taken together, the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode showed a highly electroactive surface with an enhanced electron transport efficiency, and creating a promising carbon-based hybrid electrode for biosensing application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic oxidation of DA on nanohybrid electrodes
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 5 (a) shows cyclic voltammetry measurements of bare GCE (black), NECNFs (green), and Co3O4@NECNFs with 2 h (blue) and 4 h (red) electrodeposition in the presence of 0.1 mM DA in a 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) solution. Peak current analysis of the different electrodes is shown in Fig. S11. The 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs exhibits the highest peak current and indicates improved electrocatalytic activity compared to the other electrodes. To further explore the electrocatalytic kinetics of DA oxidation on the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs, cyclic voltammetry measurements were measured for different scan rates (50 to 350 mVs−1); see Fig. S12 (a). A linear relationship is shown in Fig. S12(b) for the oxidation and reduction peak currents with scan rate and indicates that adsorption of DA is followed by a diffusion-confined electrochemical reaction [40,49]. Conversely, kinetic experiments using cobalt oxide decorated onto AECNFs (4 h Co3O4@AECNFs), see Fig. S13, show a decrease in the slope (∼32%) compared to NECNF electrodes indicating slower reaction kinetics. The fast kinetics of Co3O4@NECNFs is attributed to the synergy between the catalytic activity of the cobalt oxide nanograins and the nitrogen dopants in the carbon matrix which promote efficient electron transfer to the electrode. Fig. 5(b) shows differential pulse voltammetry measurements used to determine the limit of detection (LOD) of DA for the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs electrodes. The concentration of DA was varied between 50 nM and 100 µM and the corresponding current change was used to construct a calibration curve (Fig. 5(c)). The LOD was determined using the equation [49]:
(1)
LOD =
3
S d
S
where Sd represents the standard deviation of the blank signal (n = 3) and S is the slope of calibration plot. For the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs electrode, the LOD for DA was 9 nM which represents a relatively low LOD and wide dynamic linear range compared to other electrochemical sensors, suggesting excellent conductivity and fast charge transport along with high catalytic activity. Similarly, the LOD of using chronoamperometry (Fig. S15) was found to be ∼17 nM, reasonably at the same level of the DPV method. Table 1 shows a comparison of electrochemical sensors from this work and others in DA detection.

Because of potential endogenous interference of ascorbic acid (AA) and uric acid (UA) with DA arising from a similar oxidation potential, the selectivity of the electrochemical sensor is one of the most significant issues in practical use and was also assessed. The AA and UA interferents coexist with DA in relatively high concentrations in biological fluids; DA ranges from 0.01 to 1 µM and AA can be on the order of a few tens of µM up to 500 µM in the extracellular fluid of the central nervous system or blood plasma [75,76]. Therefore, it is necessary to test the detection of DA in the presence of higher AA and UA concentration for selectivity. Fig. 5(d) shows voltammograms for the UA (40 μM, blue line) and AA (40 μM, orange line) interferents and the current responses are negligible when detecting DA at 10 μM (pink dotted line). The superior selectivity is attributed to the chemical composition and nature of nanohybrids. Specifically, nitrogen dopants in the carbon matrix which make the surface charge more negative, providing the high selective interfacial surfaces to preferentially adsorb the positively charged DA, whereas to poorly attract or even repel the negatively charged UA and AA. To probe the fidelity and stability of the sensor, eight independently prepared sensors were compared and the electrocatalytic activity was measured over the course of a month. Fig. S13 shows that the relative standard deviation was 2.3% among the different electrodes and that only modest degradation occurred with time, i.e., ∼95% of the activity was retained, demonstrating high reproducibility and stability. Additional studies showing the differential pulse voltammetry response of the sensor to DA concentration in human serum are presented in Table S2. The nanohybrid electrochemical sensor displayed recoveries ranging from 99.6 to 101.7% with an RSD of 1.1 to 1.9% in human serum.

The electrocatalytic reaction of DA and selectivity in sensing at the Co3O4/NECNFs nanohybrid are illustrated in Fig. 6 . The introduced N dopants work together on the enhancement of the electrocatalytic nature in terms of intrinsic changes in the active surface functionality and dense surface-charged carbon matrices [77]. The formation of actively centered sites with pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen, and hydroxyl groups plays the key roles in enhancement of electroactive surface functionality with DA sensitivity and selectivity. The pyrrolic nitrogen as an active center site with lone pair of electrons at the carbon matrix surface acts as an electron donor to create strong binding with DA, the electron acceptor, to the electrode surfaces, followed with the catalyzed redox reaction at the Co3O4 and/or N-center sites, leading to sensitive and selective detection. Specifically, DA is oxidized via two-electrons and protons transfer to DA quinone (DAQ) byproducts. The catalyzed oxidation of DA on the Co3O4/NECNFs engages reaction with cobalt oxide to produce CoOOH. The mechanism of DA oxidation with Co3O4 on the N-doped ECNFs in the mild alkaline medium can be expressed as follows [3]:

The superb electrocatalytic performance can be attributed to the intermediate CoOOH-CoO2@NECNF with miscellaneous valence states of cobalt oxide and hydroxyl ions for fast kinetics, large surface-active areas, and excellent conductivity of electrode interfaces. Such a synergy effect between nitrogen doped carbon framework with cobalt oxide decoration renders rapid and sensitive DA detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-Time in situ electrochemical determination of DA secreted by PC12 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the nanohybrid electrode for monitoring the release of DA from living cells was tested. First, the biocompatibility was determined by performing a cell viability test, CCK-8 via co-culturing the 4 h Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrids and living PC12 cells with various incubation time. Following co-culturing of the cells and the nanohybrids for 8 h, the cells still exhibited good activity, indicating good biocompatibility. Fig. 7 (a) shows data from an alive/dead staining assay that corroborates this conclusion. Fig. 7(b) shows fluorescence images for the growth and evolution of alive (green) and dead (red) PC12 cells dispersed on the Co3O4@NECNFs nanohybrid surfaces after 24 h, 48 h, and 72 h. In this study, calcein AM was selected to stain all live cells to emit green fluorescence; and ethidium homodimer-1 (EthD-1) was stained with dead cells in red color. As shown in Fig. 7(b), induvial cells disperse uniformly on the surfaces of nanohybrids with very rare and negligible EthD-1 positive cells. The PC12 cells tended to aggregate together; and the number of viable cells declined after 72 h. However, a good biological activity is shown for up to 48 h. The cytotoxicity in these studies is consistent with other works which show that cobalt oxide nanoparticles are more stable, and thus less toxic, than cobalt ions [78,79]. Next, the real-time in situ amperometric response of DA secreted from living cells was monitored (Fig. 7(c)). Extracellular stimulation by K+ is known to depolarize the PC12 cell membrane by inducing an influx of Ca2+ though opening of voltage sensitive Ca2+ channel, increasing level of intracellular Ca2+, and thus inducing the release of DA [42,49,80,81]. Herein, KCl was added to the cultures to initiate DA release. Upon KCl addition a current response was observed for samples containing PC12 cells (red line), whereas no signal was observed either the cells were not present (blue line) nor with cells in the absence of K+ (black line). The concentration of secreted DA was calculated by matching the current value with the obtained calibration curve in Fig. S15 and found to be 0.14 µM, or approximately 1.7 × 10−8 µM per cells. Because the level of physiological DA in human plasma [82] is 0.23 µM and is 0.02–0.2 µM in the human brain [38,83], this nanohybrid electrode, with a sensitivity of 9.3 nM according to N/S = 3, ought to be promising for real sample applications, especially in vitro model for extracellular DA detection.

This work presents a design motif for the fabrication of Co3O4 nanograin structures on an aligned ECNF scaffold via pretreatment with N-dopants. The Co3O4@NECNF electrode was tested as an electrochemical sensor for dopamine analysis. The Co3O4@NECNF electrode displays high sensitivity over a wide range of concentrations (0.01 to 100 µM) with an excellent limit of detection (9 nM) and remarkable selectivity against interferents. These performance attributes arise from the synergy between the properties of the N-doped carbon nanofibers and the cobalt oxide for achieving high active surface area and fast charge transport. The N-doping affords nucleation sites for cobalt oxide growth during the low-current electrodeposition process and makes the electrode surface more negatively charged, leading to greater selectivity, whereas the cobalt oxide acts as the catalytic site. The electrochemical sensors exhibit good biocompatibility over 48 h and are capable of real-time detection of DA secreted from PC12 living cells with a response time in one second. More broadly, these studies point to strategies for using dopants to control surface properties and enhance selectivity of flexible bioelectronic devices for point-of-care early clinical diagnostics.
